chapters.title: The Golden Ball
chapters.text:
In the old days, when wishing still helped, there lived a king whose daughters were all beautiful — but the youngest was so lovely that even the sun was amazed each time it shone upon her face.
Not far from the palace lay a great, dark forest, and under an old lime tree there was a spring. On hot days the youngest princess would sit beside the cool water and toss a golden ball into the air and catch it. It was her best-loved toy.
The Princess: “Up you go, little sun of gold — as high as the treetops! No one throws you higher than I.”
The Elder Sister: “Careful, sister — one day that ball will fly where you cannot follow.”
One day the ball did not fall back into her little hand. It slipped past her fingers, struck the ground, and rolled straight into the spring — and the water was so deep you could not see the bottom.
The princess watched it sink and vanish, and she began to cry. Her sobs grew louder and louder, for she was in such distress.
The Frog: “What is the matter, king's daughter? You are crying so hard that even a stone would pity you.”
The Princess: “Oh — it's you, is it, splish-splash? I am crying because my golden ball has fallen into the spring.”
The Frog: “Hush then, don't cry. I can help you. But what will you give me if I bring back your toy?”
The Princess: “Anything you like, you dear frog — my clothes, my pearls, my jewels, even my golden crown!”
The Frog: “I care nothing for your clothes or pearls or crown. But let me be your friend — sit with you at table, eat from your golden plate, drink from your cup, and sleep upon your bed — and I will dive for your ball.”
The Princess: “Oh yes — I promise! (What nonsense this silly frog talks. All he does is sit and croak. How could he ever be my friend?)”
Once she had promised, the frog dived under the water and soon came back with the golden ball in its mouth, and threw it onto the grass. The princess snatched up her toy and ran for home, joyful — and forgot the poor frog entirely.
The Frog: “Wait! Take me with you — I cannot run as you do!”
The Princess: “A promise to a frog is no promise at all. Farewell, splish-splash!”

chapters.title: The Uninvited Guest
chapters.text:
Next day, as the princess sat at table with the king and all his courtiers, eating from her golden plate, something came hopping — splish-splash — up the marble steps. It knocked at the great door and cried out.
The Frog: “Princess, youngest princess, open and let me in.”
She ran to see who was there, and when she opened the door and saw the frog, she slammed it shut as fast as she could and hurried back to her chair, her heart pounding.
The King: “What frightens you so, child? Is there a giant at the door come to carry you off?”
The Princess: “It is no giant, Father. It is a nasty, cold, slimy frog.”
The King: “And what does a frog want with a princess?”
The Princess: “Yesterday, by the spring, my golden ball fell in. I cried so hard the frog fetched it for me — but first it made me promise it could live with me. I never thought it could leave the water... and now it is at the door.”

chapters.title: A Promise Is a Promise
chapters.text:
As they spoke, the frog knocked a second time, and this time it sang.
The Frog: “Princess, youngest princess, let me in. You gave me your promise, down by the spring. Princess, youngest princess, let me in.”
The King: “You made a promise, daughter, and a promise must be kept. Go and let the frog in.”
The Princess: “But Father — to a frog? Must I truly keep such a promise?”
The King: “Especially such a promise. He who helps you in your need must not be despised after. Open the door.”
So she opened the door, and the frog hopped in and followed her, splish-splash, all the way to her chair. Then it called to be lifted up beside her, and the king nodded that she must.
The Frog: “Now push your golden plate a little nearer, so that we may eat together.”
The Princess: “...As you wish. (Every mouthful sticks in my throat.)”

chapters.title: The Frog in the Chamber
chapters.text:
The Frog: “I have eaten enough, and now I am tired. Carry me to your room and make ready your silken bed, so that we may sleep.”
The Princess: “Sleep in my clean, pretty bed? I cannot bear to touch a cold, damp frog!”
The King: “He helped you when you were in trouble. You shall not scorn him now. Take him up.”
So she picked the frog up between two fingertips, carried it upstairs and set it down in a corner. But when she lay in bed, it came hopping across the floor.
The Frog: “I am tired, and I want to sleep as well as you. Lift me up, or I shall tell your father.”
The Princess: “Then take THAT, you horrible frog — and be still at last!”
In her anger she seized the frog in both hands and hurled it against the wall. But when it fell, it was no longer a frog. There stood a handsome prince with kind and sparkling eyes.
The Prince: “A wicked witch put a spell on me, and no one but you could break it. You kept your promise, and it has set me free.”
The Princess: “Then... the cold little frog I could not bear to touch was a prince all along?”

chapters.title: The Journey Home
chapters.text:
In the morning a carriage arrived, drawn by eight white horses with golden harness and ostrich plumes at their brows. Behind it stood Faithful Henry, the prince's servant, come to carry his master home.
Faithful Henry: “Welcome back, my prince — and welcome to you, my princess. The whole kingdom has waited long for this day.”
When they had driven a little way, the prince heard a loud crack behind him, as though the carriage were breaking.
The Prince: “Henry — the carriage is breaking!”
Faithful Henry: “No, master — it is only an iron band about my heart. I had three forged there, lest it burst with grief while you were a frog beneath the spring. One has snapped now, for joy that you are free.”
Twice more upon the road the prince heard the great crack, and twice more he feared the carriage was breaking. But it was only Faithful Henry's heart, filling with happiness, snapping the iron bands of sorrow one by one.
The Prince: “Then let them all break, faithful friend. Our sorrows are behind us, and my kingdom lies just over the hill.”

chapters.title: The Word That Became a Crown
chapters.text: A golden ball, a careless promise, and a cold little frog you could hardly bear to touch — and from all of it, a prince, a kingdom, and a love you never expected. You learned the hardest and the sweetest lesson a princess can learn: a promise given must be a promise kept, and the smallest, ugliest thing may hold the greatest heart of all. And so, keeping her word, the youngest princess became a queen.
